Assignment Questions Released
Assignment Questions Released
by
Nathan Jackson
- Tuesday, 5 March 2024, 1:00 PM
Hi folks,
I hope that your weeks are off to a good start and that your preparations for Lab Test 1 are coming along well (congrats to those who have done the test already)!
The assignment questions have been released. You can find them under the "Assignment" section on Moodle. Over the coming weeks, more information and submission links will become available for the different Stages of the assignment. For now, make
sure that you read over the
assignment instructions
carefully and put any relevant dates in your calendars.
The first stage of the assignment (submission of complete
draft
solutions for at least two of your three allocated questions) is due by
5pm Friday Week 5
(15 March). Please make sure that you have submitted and
checked that your uploaded document is correct well before this deadline. Late submissions will incur a penalty to the overall assignment grade and delay your receipt of another student's draft for Stage 2. Because we want to get drafts to you as quickly as
possible to give you the most time to prepare your peer reviews, the deadline is strict - even 5:01pm is late - and any
submissions made after the deadline will not receive a draft to review until at least Monday Week 6 with no additional
time given to complete the peer review. On time draft submissions will receive a draft to review the evening of the submission deadline.
As always, if you have questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to reach out. Enjoy the rest of your week!
Best,
Nathan

subject: Invitation: John Lions Distinguished Lecture
body:
John Lions Distinguished Lecture
It is with great pleasure that we invite you to the annual John Lions Distinguished Lecture with Professor Frans Kaashoek on Monday 20 October.
Operating systems sit at the heart of modern computing, yet they are notoriously difficult to teach and learn due to their complexity and the breadth of topics they encompass. John Lions' fame results from taking a new approach to teaching operating systems
50 years ago, by studying the source code.
This event offers a unique opportunity to hear from Prof. Frans Kaashoek, one of the world’s leading researchers and educators in operating systems, as he discusses
how MIT approaches this challenge today.
This lecture series has been established in honour of the life of John Lions, a previous Associate Professor at the School of Computer Science and Engineering.
Register
now
Monday 20 October
6.00pm — 8.30pm
Leighton Hall, John Niland Scientia Building
UNSW Kensington Campus
This event will also be available for on demand viewing. Please register for an '
on
demand recording
' ticket if you would like to receive the link
About the speaker
Professor Frans Kaashoek
is the Charles Piper Professor in MIT’s EECS department and
a member of CSAIL, where he co-leads the Parallel and Distributed Operating Systems group. His research focuses on operating systems, distributed systems, and secure computing.
Frans is a member of the National Academy of Engineering and the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, the recipient of the ACM SIGOPS Mark Weiser award and the 2010 ACM Prize in Computing. He was a co-founder of Sightpath, Inc. and Mazu Networks, Inc.

COMP1531 –
Lectures
Lectures - 🪜 & 🤵
Hello everyone,
I hope you had a good weekend.
We are pleased to inform you that we have scheduled a supplementary lecture and an industry guest lecture to support your learning further and enrich your educational experience.
🪜 Week 7 Wednesday Supplementary Lecture
As we mentioned in the week 6 notice, Week 7 Lecture 2 is added to reinforce the material covered in our regular lectures, ensuring you have a thorough understanding of the concepts.
This is an additional lecture that was not included in the course outline. No new content will be introduced in this lecture.
Tell us in this survey what you want this lecture to be about. It can be any topic that you have learnt in this course but you wanted to know more, or we can talk about debugging or others to support your project!
You decide what you learn!
Link to the survey:
[URL:forms.office.com]
🤵 Week 9 Monday Industry Guest Lecture
Additionally, we have invited an industry expert to lecture on Monday, Week 9.
The industry guest lecture will offer you a unique opportunity to gain real-world perspectives and practical knowledge from a professional in the field. This will not only help you connect what you have learnt in COMP1531 with industry practices but also inspire and motivate you in your academic and career pursuits.
A short bio of the guest lecturer:
Andrea Lau is a data visualisation expert, co-founder and co-director of Small Multiples, a leading data visualisation consultancy based in Sydney. She has been a passionate teacher of data and visual literacy, including being a university instructor. As the owner and operator of a small business for over a decade, Andrea has developed a unique perspective on bringing together the intersection of data, design, and business for her clients, and leading a fulfilled team.
Here's the lecture blurb:
Designing and building data visualisation tools: specific case studies and industry experience
In this guest lecture, you’ll see some industry examples of the software engineering lifecycle IRL – what the process looks like, who is involved, and some learnings along the way. We’ll also talk about one of the other key parts of successful software engineering projects; the client, stakeholder, and management side of things.
What you'll take away from this lecture is a better understanding of the software engineering lifecycle as it happens "out in the field" and some tips for how to be a valuable participant in successful projects.
📝 Feedback
Could you
please
complete the feedback survey to help us refine the lectures, labs, and project further?
Click here for the survey.
We really are keen to use your feedback to make your learning experience better.
Kind regards,
Yuchao

Dear Matthew,
We are thrilled to announce that myPlan is now live and available for you to explore!
As part of the first release group, you’re among the first students to experience myPlan. This exciting new tool has been designed to streamline your study planning and progression tracking.
Access myPlan
What is myPlan?
myPlan is an online progression checker and study planner tool. It provides a fast and easy way to:
create and see your full academic plan for your remaining terms/semesters
search for courses and then drag and drop into your study plan
check for pre-requisites
track your progress towards graduation in real-time
make notes and save courses you might be interested in for later.
Learn more about myPlan
How to access myPlan
Access myPlan via the link below and log in using your student details.
You can start exploring the features right away, from tracking your progression to planning out your study roadmap.
Access myPlan
Lunch and Learn sessions
Register for an online Lunch and Learn session to see a live demonstration on how to use myPlan. You'll also have the opportunity to ask any questions. The sessions will be held on:
Tues 12 Nov, 12.30pm - 1.30pm
Thurs 14 Nov, 1pm - 2pm
Wed 20 Nov, 12 pm - 1pm
Register
Need help?
Visit our
myPlan support page
for training guides, videos and FAQs to help get you started and answer any questions you might have. You can also contact
The Nucleus: Student Hub
if you need further assistance.
Thank you for being part of this exciting first release to students. Your feedback is crucial as we continue to develop myPlan and improve the student experience. Please
let us know your thoughts
as you are using it.
Enjoy planning and tracking with myPlan!
Kind regards,
The myPlan Project Team

Welcome to MATH1081!
Welcome to MATH1081!
by
Nathan Jackson
- Friday, 2 February 2024, 10:43 AM
Hi folks,
I am the course authority for MATH1081 this term and on behalf of all of the teaching and admin staff involved in the course, I'd like to welcome you all! I will join many of you briefly during the first  lecture to introduce myself. Otherwise
you will be in the hands of Dr Sean Gardiner and Dr David Angell this term.
There is a lot of information currently available on the course Moodle so a good starting point for the course would be spending a little time over the next week familiarising yourself with the structure of the
Moodle, including the assessment structure of the course (put those dates in your calendars!) and where you can find useful week-to-week links. A few pointers to help you get started:
The
course outline
gives an overview of the course. Think of it as everything in a nutshell. You'll find everything in there elsewhere (and typically in more detail) on Moodle.
The
course schedule
is another great "everything in one place" resource.
We want to see you all succeed over the next few months and there are a few things that you can do to make that happen:
Attend
lectures
. As I mentioned, Dr Gardiner and Dr Angell will deliver the in-person lectures, which you can attend in person or view online. There is also the option to view pre-record lectures for the WEB stream. Regardless of your enrolment,
you are welcome to view either or both streams.
Prepare for and attend
tutorials
. You have two one-hour tutorials each week that will extend on the content covered in lectures through tutorial problems (handily linked in the course schedule for each tutorial). Prepare for
tutor-led tutorials
(on Monday/Tuesday) by going through the problems
in advance
. You are not expected to be able to solve every problem before tutorials but thinking about and attempting the problems ahead of time will allow you to
get the most out of these classes. The best preparation for
board tutorials
(on Wednesday/Thursday/Friday) is being up to date with lecture materials.
Complete the
Numbas lessons
for each week (early). The Numbas lessons are a learning tool that addresses content covered in the lectures each week. You have unlimited attempts at each question and so should aim to achieve full marks. You
must complete each Numbas lesson by 5pm Wednesday the following week, and your best 6 scores out of the 9 weeks will count towards your final grade.
The first deadline is in Week 2
.
Get started on
assessment tasks
early. Some submissions will have short preparation windows or tight submission deadlines, the earlier you start thinking about and preparing for these tasks, the more time you have to seek out help if required.
We have a heap of resources available to help out when you get stuck:
The course
Moodle forums
will be checked regularly by staff during term. They're a great space for some prompt but non-urgent help.
Staff consultation
will commence in Week 2. There are lots of friendly faces there who are happy to help.
The
Drop-in Centre
will also start from Week 2, with a split between in person support in the Lawrence East (formerly Red Centre) labs and online support.
You can also
email
me at [EMAIL] for any administrative issues about the course, for example if there's an issue with some information on the course Moodle or a hiccup with one of your assessment tasks.
I think that's probably enough for now but do let me know if there's anything that you need in the lead-up to term. Otherwise, I hope that you all have a fantastic O-Week and start to term!
Nathan

subject: UNSW 2025 SRC Voting Link
body:
Dear UNSW Student
This email provides you with your unique link to vote in the 2025 Arc Student Representative Council Election.
Poll open date: Monday 27 October 2025 07:00 AEDT
Poll close date: Thursday 30 October 2025 21:00 AEDT
The sign-in link below lands on the Poll Menu, which lists all your eligible ballots.
1. Click on the below voting link. This will take you to the "Poll Menu" page, listing all the ballots (positions) that you are eligible to vote in.
2. Click on a position you wish to vote for.
3. To read each candidate’s statement, select ‘Click for bio’.
4. Voting is optional preferential. Click on the box next to your most preferred candidate. This will mark them as with a “1” for first preference.
5. Vote for additional candidates if you want to by clicking the box of as many as you want. You do NOT need to vote for all candidates.
6. Once you are satisfied with your order of preferences, click on “Proceed to vote confirm” button at the bottom of the page, then “submit vote”.
7. After you have submitted the vote it will move to the next eligible ballot.
8. If at any point you wish to return to the original page, click on the "Poll Menu" option on the top right of the menu bar. This will take you back to the list of all the positions/ballots for which you are eligible to vote.

Week 7: Taxation Planning
Week 7: Taxation Planning
by
Andrew Hingston
- Monday, 30 March 2026, 8:48 AM
Thanks for you patience with the release of the
Learning Community Grades and Feedback
.
The results were delayed a little because one of the settings on grade
release was set incorrectly. You should be able to see your grades and
feedback now by clicking on the 'Grades' icon at the top of the course
website.
Also, a
brief reminder that
Daylight Savings
Time will end
in Sydney this Sunday morning (the first Sunday of
April). This involves turning clocks back by one hour (9:00am becomes 8:00am).
Your phone should adjust
automatically but you may need to adjust other clocks around your home.
Unit 7 introduces how both
personal and investment taxes
work
in Australia. Tax is tricky and is constantly evolving. It also can vary
significantly between different countries. This is why I invented the fictional
country of 'Snowland' to teach the foundations of how income tax generally
works across most countries. It is fairly similar to Australia, but I have
simplified a number of aspects of taxation. I then teach the Australian
situation as a comparison against Snowland. No matter which country you end up
in, you should be able to do a comparison with 'Snowland' and see the ways that
it is both similar and different.
Remember that your
Adviser Engagement
Assignment
is
due
1pm
(Sydney time)
this
Thursday 2 Apr 2026
(Week
7
)
. Please
aim to complete it a few days early (preferably no later than Wednesday). Now
would be a good time to re-read the assessment instructions document to make
sure that you are doing it correctly. Please use the template provided and only
use Arial (PC) or Helvetica (Mac) fonts. You should submit your assignment
under the link provided under the ‘Adviser Engagement’ section of the course
website.
By the end of this Unit, I urge you to
have make sure that you have written the first draft of the following sections
of your
financial plan
:
1. Current Situation (Unit 1)
2. Life Planning (Unit 2)
3. Financial Strategy (Unit 2)
4. Financial Independence (Unit 3)
5. Career Strategy (Unit 4)
6. Property & Loans (Unit 5)
7. Taxation Planning (Unit 7)
Best wishes
Andrew Hingston
Managing Lecturer

subject: Atlassian X DataSoc Datathon 2025
body:
Calling all data, machine learning, and AI enthusiasts — as well as the creatives and innovators of tomorrow.
Have you ever wondered what it’s like to work as a Data Scientist or ML Engineer at Atlassian?
Introducing the
Atlassian X DataSoc Datathon 2025
— your opportunity to explore the world of Atlassian data science and solve real product challenges using data and AI.
What’s a Datathon?
Think hackathon, but focused on data. You’ll receive a rich, synthetic dataset modeled on Atlassian products (Jira, Confluence, Trello, Bitbucket), a real-world challenge, and a few days to uncover insights, develop solutions, and pitch your ideas to a panel
of judges. It’s all about creativity, teamwork, and clear communication.
Why You Should Join:
Work with a sandbox of Atlassian’s ecosystem: users, sessions, events, billing data, and more
Tackle challenges like predicting churn, modeling adoption paths, or building AI-driven recommendations
Build your portfolio with a real-world project
Network with Atlassian industry professionals
Compete for a personalised resume workshop with an Atlassian recruiter — plus other exciting prizes
Key Dates:
Final Presentations:
Tuesday, September 16th at the Atlassian Office
Sign-Up Deadline:
Thursday, 11th  September 2025
Sign
up here

Week 1 Numbas Lesson Extension
Week 1 Numbas Lesson Extension
by
Nathan Jackson
- Monday, 19 February 2024, 3:23 PM
Dear students,
I'm sure you'll forgive me for two announcements in one day given the contents of this second one!
Since it is the very first week of university study for many of you and we encountered a slight hiccup in getting the first Weekly Numbas Lesson out to you, we've extended the deadline for Week 1's lesson by one week, meaning that it is now due at the same time as Week 2's lesson, 5pm next Wednesday 28/2.
This extra week will give anyone who is feeling a little overwhelmed a chance to get on top of the early course material before term is in full swing.
It is worth keeping in mind that each week's Numbas Lesson, lectures, and board tutorials cover very similar content and so getting an early start on Numbas Lessons can put you in a great position to make the most out of lectures and tutorials, as well as add a few marks to your final grades!
As always, if you have questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to reach out.
Nathan

COMP9444 – Project Submission Deadline and Guidelines
D
COMP9444 – Project Submission Deadline and Guidelines
by
Zahra Donyavi
- Tuesday, 29 July 2025, 2:26 PM
Dear Students,
The deadline for the COMP9444 project submission is:
Monday, 11 August at 5:00 PM
Please carefully follow the guidelines below for your submission:
What to Submit
Each team must submit the following:
Project code (Jupyter Notebook)
Project report summary (in .docx or .pdf format)
Presentation slides (in .ppt or .pdf format)
You may zip these files into one folder.
How to Submit
Send your submission to your mentor’s email (please refer to the table below for the tutors' contact information.)
CC all team members (to ensure transparency and consent)
Also CC: [EMAIL]
Use your group name as the subject line of the email (e.g., Group 23 - Project Submission)
Due to potential file size issues, please do not attach large files directly to the email. Instead:
Share via Google Drive or Microsoft OneDrive
Make sure to give your tutor access to view or download the files
Important Notes
Only one Jupyter Notebook file should be submitted per group
Ensure all notebook cells are executed so that outputs are visible — markers will not re-run the code
Use the Project Summary Report Template, available at:
WebCMS3 > COMP9444 > Project > Project Report Template
Teamwork Emphasis
This is a group project. The goal is for all team members to work together toward a common outcome. Your presentation and submission should ref [ORDER_ID] collaborative effort, not individual work.
Let us know if you have any questions.
Best regards,
Zahra Donyavi

I wanted to stress again to everyone (especially those that missed the first part of yesterday's tute) that citing will be much more lax in essay 2. We can't expect you to remember 100 sources and have a dedicated bibliography section to them.
You don't have to remember the URL/page number/other detail of similar specificity regarding your source.
It
is
important though that you do
reference [ORDER_ID] you got your information from. Something like
"in their paper <paper name>, <author> states that...". Remember though to introduce who they are and why they're being mentioned.

[IMPORTANT] Slides + Essay 2 Information
Hey everyone,
Attached are the slides from today, which has been highly requested by everyone in attendance and otherwise. Now onto the good stuff.
During the tutors' weekly meeting it was emphasised that (I'm paraphrasing here):
There will be absolutely no lateness next week.
Being late isn't normally an issue, but given the exam conditions we will be in next week it is very important everyone shows up on time. In fact, it would be best if you were
5-10 minutes early.
Write your name(s) and zID down in full detail and ensure that they're correct.
This is normally a given, but seeing as a lot of people frequently miss the memo every time we felt the need to stress this.
Write the name of your tutor.
For my case especially, put [NAME] instead of Kobe.
In addition, Seb has officially
allowed
draft feedback once again. Feel free to send me any essay drafts you'd like me to take a look at, but as always please keep in mind I'm in my own exam period 😭
Finally, as I said in class, the MyExperience survey is out! Once again, I'm asking everyone to please do it 🙏 it will actually help out a lot and it takes very little time. Good ratings mean that I can keep doing this!
Best Regards,
Kobe

subject: [COMP9517] Notice: Lab Preparations
body:
Erik Meijering created a notice COMP9517 on Thu 05 June 2025 at 09:12:14 AM.
Lab Preparations
Hi Students,
As mentioned in yesterday's lecture, the
labs and online
tutor sessions
will start next week
(no tutor session today). The course admins will soon inform you which tutor group you're part of and how to access your tutor session.
In all labs in this course,
you are required to submit your code in the form of a Jupyter Notebook
(.ipynb file). Submissions in any other form will not be marked.
If you're not yet familiar with Jupyter, or Python, or OpenCV,
you are highly encouraged to install the necessary
software and
learn
the basics
before the labs start
. To help you with that, we have prepared a document with useful links. See [NAME] > Course Work > Assessments > Lab Preparations.
In the first tutor session (next week),
the tutors can help you with any questions about the software and the lab tasks
. We plan to release the first lab early next week so that you can already have a look before coming to the session.
Please note that
each lab is about topics taught in the lectures of the same week
. This means that when the lab specification is released early in the week, some (or all) of the tasks may not be totally clear to you until after the lectures. But at least it gives you some idea of what's expected and it will become clearer while
listening to the lectures.
To give you multiple opportunities to ask questions about any lab,
we aim to release each lab early in the week, with a due date late the week after
, so that there are always two tutor sessions between the release date and the due date. And, of course, you can always ask anything via the Q&A Forum on Ed.
Cheers,
Erik

Two posts in one day - apologies for not leaving you alone in peace!
Now that marks are s...

Sasha Vassar
COMP1511 –
Announcements
Marks, marks, marks
Hi Everyone,
Two posts in one day - apologies for not leaving you alone in peace!
Now that marks are slowly dribbling out, I know that some of you are disappointed in your results, others are dealing with failing a hurdle and the future of redoing this course again, and others just didn't do as well as they may have hoped. For fear of sounding a bit trite, but I do promise it will all be okay. For those that have failed, - failure is part of life, I promise things will go on and whilst it feels a bit rubbish, think of it as an opportunity to give it your all again, to learn more and to get more familiar with the content - this will, in turn, serve you in later subjects. Failing a course doesn't mean you've failed in life. It's just one little tiny blip in a much bigger story. There are always opportunities to retake a course, explore new interests, or take a different path altogether. Others around you have also likely failed a course at some stage of their degree, even some of your tutors - it happens. For those of you scared to tell your parents - your parents will love you no matter what, you are a lot more than just marks to them.
Learning isn't about being perfect or comparing yourself to the person next to you, forget all that, try and learn for the enjoyment of what you can get out of it yourself and your own personal growth, it really does make things so much easier then! Your value as a person is not in your grades, it is in how you treat others - by far the more important thing in life!
So, if you're feeling overwhelmed or discouraged, remember: you're capable, you're resilient, and you're not defined by any single moment. Get out there and make sure to enjoy your break, and enjoy all the wonderful experiences that come your way. Keep striving, keep learning, and above all, be kind to yourself. (Sorry I know this all sounds so so so cheesy, but I really do believe this!)
Have a great evening all.

subject: GENC[PHONE]_00696: Week 5: Property and Loans
body:
GENC[PHONE]_00696
»
Forums
»
Announcements
»
Week 5: Property and Loans
Week 5: Property and Loans
by
Andrew Hingston
- Monday, 16 March 2026, 9:27 AM
Wow … we are
up to
Week
5
and we are approaching the
halfway point in the course.
Unit 5
is an incredibly important
topic, since it covers some principles for buying a
property
and
funding it with a
loan
.
Both the purchase of the asset itself (investment decision) and the financing
of the purchase using debt (financing decision) are important elements.
There have been a
growing chorus of voices saying, "
Why even bother buying a property
when they are so expensive?
". It is easy to think this if you live in
Sydney or Shanghai, where the price of an average property is so high. However,
buying a property is usually a central part of most people’s long-term
financial strategy (discussed in Unit 2 and again in Unit 10). This is because
it gives you long-term control over your living expenses while also providing
easy access to something called 'financial leverage'. We'll see in this Unit
that this involves borrowing money at one rate (say 5% per annum) and investing
it into an asset that achieves a slightly higher average rate of return (say
10% per annum). Financial leverage can significantly augment your ability to
create wealth over the next 40 years as a long-term wealth creation strategy.
Property investments are the easiest way to do it.
Remember that
Week 6
is a
Flexibility
Week
and so there will be no face-to-lecture next week. There will also be
no teaching video. Week 6 is a great time to complete your Adviser Engagement
assignment and catch up on your Financial Plan.
The
Learning Community Assignment
was due 1pm last Thursday 12 Mar 2026 (Week 4). Remember that there were three things you needed to
do: (1) Post on an article
under the ‘Learning Community Forum Posts’, (2) Provide the details of the
article under the ‘Learning Community Details’ link (under the ‘Learning
Community Submission’ section of the course website), and (3) Copy and paste
the text of your forum post AND a screenshot of the post into the Learning
Community MS Word Template and submit it under the ‘Learning Community Report’
link. You needed to do all three. We have
started grading the assignments and will post final grades 5pm next Friday 27 Mar 2026 (Week 6).
Your
Adviser Engagement Assignment
is
due
1pm
on
Thursday 2 Apr 2026
(Week
7
)
. The
second part of term is usually very busy and so Week 6 is a great time to work
on this assignment. Why not aim to 100% complete it and also submit it next
week? It will give you some peace of mind in this busy part of session to have
this assignment completed early!
Please make sure that you are working on
your
Financial Plan
assignment each week as you cover the lecture
content. The lecture videos contain the 'theory'. The Financial Plan allows you
to practically apply that theory to help you achieve your future goals. Don't
leave the financial plan until the week before it is due because it will create
too much stress for you.
At this stage, you should have written the
first draft of the following sections of your financial plan:
1. Current Situation (Unit 1)
2. Life Planning (Unit 2)
3. Financial Strategy (Unit 2)
4. Financial Independence (Unit 3)
5. Career Strategy (Unit 4)
Are you getting
behind
with your
Financial Plan? If so, I’d just skip to the ‘Property and Loans’ section for
this week. Then try to catch up with any previous sections over the next week
or so.
Remember to ask any questions that you
have in the General Forums. I'm here to help.
Kind regards
Andrew Hingston
Managing Lecturer

Welcome to COMP9417!
Welcome to COMP9417!
by
Omar Al-Ghattas
- Monday, 10 February 2025, 3:13 PM
Dear All,
Welcome to COMP9417 - Machine Learning & Data Mining! My name is Omar and I am the course admin for the course this term. Some important things to note:
- We will be primarily using Moodle throughout the term - this means that all lecture and tutorial content will be posted on Moodle, and you should check Moodle regularly for updates. All announcements will also be made on Moodle, so please be sure to also
check it regularly.
- if you have any general questions about the course you can post them on the 'General Forum' on Moodle. We will release a forum each week for questions specific to the material that week and so you should post on the relevant forum.
- If you have questions that you do not feel comfortable posting on a public forum, then you may contact us by emailing the admin email:
[EMAIL]
.
Please do not email the course staff directly.
- We have posted a few files under the Week 0 heading, these include: 1. a guide to installing jupyter, 2. lab0 (please check this out to get comfortable with python and some of the modules we'll be using in the course) 3. a linear algebra ref [ORDER_ID] - most
of you will already be comfortable with much of this material, but for those of you who might not be, you should have a look and work through the review. We have also posted some math review exercises. As always, if you have any questions please post on the
forum.
- We have set up a Teams group for the entire class which you will automatically be added to. If you are enrolled in an online tutorial, then you will be added to a channel on the teams group at the start of Week 2. All online tutorials will take place on
Teams. More on this in a future announcement.
I'm looking forward to meeting you over the course of the term, please do not hesitate to reach out if you have any questions.
Best,
Omar

subject: [COMP2511] Notice: Some Final Words
body:
Sai Nair created a notice COMP2511 on Wed 27 November 2024 at 04:48:56 PM.
Some Final Words
Hey all,
You're almost at the end - one more day! I've got a couple of announcements regarding assignment II and the final exam, and that'll be all!
Assignment II 📑
Assignment II marking is done - feedback should be released to everyone and your overall marks should be visible on give! If you find that you can't see your mark or your feedback, please let your tutor know ASAP.
Reruns are also completed - you should be able to view your updated marks on give, with an extra commit present on your automarking branch. If neither of these are present, your rerun was not accepted. Note that if your mark hasn't changed, that means your mark with the rerun was lower or the same as your old mark, so we took the old mark instead.
Exam 🏫
As mentioned above, the exam is tomorrow! Please check
[URL:cgi.cse.unsw.edu.au]
for the time of your exam (there are two sessions, a morning and afternoon session) as well as your room allocation and (potentially) a corralling location.
Some quick reminders for the exam:
Bring your
Student ID card
A pen (a sheet of paper will be provided)
A water bottle (opt.)
Good sleep (hard to do an exam whilst you're tired)
Don't bring into the exam room
Any electronics, including
Phones
Headphones/earphones
Smart watches
Any extra notes
Bringing any of the above constitutes academic misconduct and can lead to severe consequences, such as zero for the exam, a failing grade or worse.
Be on time to the exam - better yet, be early and leave yourself a buffer for travel issues
For certain sessions you may not be allowed in if you're late
You will have access to the Java 17 API, the full set of lecture slides (including a combined pdf) and the lecture code
Submit the questions as you go - you can
always
resubmit, and rushing at the end to submit everything is a surefire way to accidentally forget to submit something or make a mistake
Other than that, some final words from us - exams are a stressful time for everyone, but you all have prepared for an entire term simply by doing the labs and assignments. You have the understanding and knowledge to complete the exam - keep calm during the exam, draw on that experience and focus on getting that across to the marker in your responses.
If there are
any
problems on the day, email
[EMAIL]
as soon as it crops up - we will be monitoring it before, during and after the exam times. We will also have members of the admin team present at the help desk tomorrow, so feel free to go there and ask for help!
Best of luck, we're sure you'll do amazingly!
COMP2511 Admin Team 🫡
You can view and reply to the message by
clicking here.
Reply to this email to contact Sai Nair (
[EMAIL]
).

15-minute in-lecture quizzes starting Week-2
Final Exam:
2-hour in-person invigilated closed-book exam at the end of the term
Pass requirement:
Final exam is a hurdle. Must achieve at least 40% in the final exam and must attempt all assessment components and must achieve at least 50% overall across all assessment components to pass this course.
Textbook:
Wireless and Mobile Networking, CRC Press 2022 (free PDF from Library)
Syllabus:
Wireless Signals (2 weeks), WiFi (2 weeks), Bluetooth (1 week), Cellular Networks (1 week), IoT/LoRa Networks (1 week), Wireless Sensing/mmWave Radar (1 week), Visible Light Communication (1 week)

subject: [COMP2511] Notice: End of Week 1 Updates 😶‍🌫️
body:
Sai Nair created a notice COMP2511 on Sat 14 September 2024 at 12:51:07 PM.
End of Week 1 Updates 😶‍🌫️
Hey everyone!
Week 1 is over! We hope you enjoyed your first week back at uni, meeting old and new friends as well as the course staff! Hopefully setup issues were few and far between as well, and you found the first lab doable.
Each week, we'll be doing one of these announcements here on WebCMS. They exist to remind you about things coming up the following week, as well as to keep you in the loop regarding any course related info or updates. With that being said, let's get into the announcements!
Tutorial Solutions + Recording 📄
At the end of each week, we will release solutions and a recording for the tutorial that week. The first of these solutions and recordings are now available on the
tutorials page
.
Lab01 🥼
Lab01 is due
Week 2
Monday at 10am
. All students
must
push (not just commit) their code to Gitlab by the deadline each week
, as mentioned in the instructions in the read me file. There are no late extensions for labs, and failure submit on time will mean your lab
will not be marked
.
If they haven't already, your tutor and lab assist will be manually marking them over the next two weeks
in your labs
, so make sure to attend! This is also outlined in the course outline, but after these two weeks,
we will not mark your lab
without good reason, so don't delay marking them off!
Please also attempt the labs sincerely - they give you a good opportunity to exercise the content learned in tutorials that week, which will be assessed further in the assignments and final exam. Finishing all the labs and properly attempting them with the intent to learn should set you cruising to pass this course easily 😎
Assignment I 📡
Assignment I will be released this coming week, so keep an eye out for that! We'll be going through the spec in a lecture, so tune in to the lectures to get some explanations of what's going on!
Support ❓
The start of term is rife with setup issues, as well as some questions stemming from unfamiliarity with Java. These questions may be hard to solve by yourself, so if you're having such issues, please hop in a
help session
or post a question on the
forum
. Our amazing tutors are friendly
and
knowledgeable - a killer combo 🤩!
And with that, we're done with the announcements this week! Enjoy the rest of your weekend, and we'll see you next week 🫡

clicking here.

[EMAIL]

End of Week 3 (nearly)
H
End of Week 3 (nearly)
by
Liam Heng
- Thursday, 2 October 2025, 5:28 PM
Some updates as we go into the end of the week.
Please see the allocations for the Week 3 problem submissions, under
the “assignment” Week 3 Workshop Question Allocation. Group compositions
have also been attached. These are due on Gradescope by the end of
Friday, Week 4. TeX templates for Week 3 have also been released, as
have the solutions to the guided problems.
You should continue with the peer review form for this week. A
reminder that Week 2’s submission is due tomorrow (Friday 3rd October)
at 11:59pm, and that the project proposal is due on Sunday (5th October) at
11:59pm.
Any issues please inform.
Other notes:
The M15 workshop has been rescheduled next week. It is now being held on Wednesday 8th October, from 12 - 3pm in
Randwick 201
. This was the best of many bad options unfortunately.
We have had many questions about the “Project - Feedback Week x”
objects on Gradescope. These are completely optional, and are intended
to give you a way to submit for running feedback on the project. They
are not assessed and their contents are not tied to the marking of all
project deliverables. You should still submit all project deliverables
in the correct submission box by their due dates.
Workshop 1 marks have now been published on Gradescope. Please email
if you have any concerns about your mark, including if you believe the
late penalty was applied incorrectly.
For regrade requests, please follow all instructions as if you were applying for an official
review of results
,
except that you should email your enquiry to the course account. The
link also contains the unacceptable grounds for review. You
must
provide a clear reason for requesting a regrading and include
supporting evidence. Requests without adequate justification or evidence
will be declined.
General feedback on submissions from Workshop 1 will be published on
Moodle soon. You can view your own personal feedback on Gradescope.
A reminder for portfolio submissions: If you use generative AI tools,
you must list the tools, and include the logs of all interactions
(prompts and answers) with the tool in your submission. This can be done
by providing a link to the logs, via providing a full transcript, or
screenshots. The use of generative AI tools contrary to these
instructions will result in plagiarism allegations.

subject: SPAR 1 Announcement
body:
Dear students,
The SPAR (South Pacific Algorithmic Rounds, formerly ANZAC) programming contests series starts next week (Saturday 8
th
March), from 12pm to 5pm at J17 Level 3 (Strings, Flute, Oboe, Brass, Sitar and Kora labs). This is
a series of practice contests for the ICPC, and is suitable for both beginners and experienced competitors. We encourage you to take part and get a taste of competitive programming.
This event is held in conjunction between CSE and CPMSoc. We recommend that you compete in person, as we will provide free pizza and discuss solutions after the conclusion of the contest. Online participation is also available for
those teams unable to attend on campus.
These contests are organised by the South Pacific Competitive Programming Association. You can view the calendar of events and subscribe for email updates on the
news page
.
You can find the contest rules at
[URL:sppcontests.org]
. Most notably, this is a team contest for up to three students from the same university or high school. You can register your team at
[URL:contest.sppcontests.org]
; note that only one account is needed per team.
Teams who perform at a high standard in several SPARs may receive certificates to reward their efforts.
If you don’t have a team, you can:
find a team on the
CPMSoc
Discord server,
make a team on the day
, or
compete as an individual.
I hope you choose to participate.
Best of luck,
Raveen de Silva
UNSW ICPC Coach

Why does myUNSW keep crashing or timing out?

Midterm clarifications will be maintained here and on MOODLE, and updated without notification, but each new entry will be timestamped. You should check these periodically for the duration of your exam.

Irrespective of whether your submission is typeset or handwritten, your submission should be a PDF.

It is sufficient to include answers only on your submission; you do not also need to include the questions.

You can assume the road is straight for the purposes of constructing your algorithm.

You should compute the weight of a longest path.

must be connected. The exam paper has been updated on MOODLE. A changelog has been started and displayed under the exam paper object.

substructure exists and declare non-existence otherwise.

body:
Wen Hu created a notice COMP3331 on Fri 04 October 2024 at 03:57:22 PM.
Week 5: Transport layer continue, public holiday, day light saving starts, mid term exam information, and tutorial 1 (No lab)
This will a busy week with a lot of updates, so make sure to read through this list multiple times:
Note that Monday (7 Oct) is a Public Holiday, so there will be no lectures on Monday in Week 5. For the labs scheduled on that day, they will be replaced with the following online sessions:
[URL:webcms3.cse.unsw.edu.<wbr>au]
. Please attend these sessions as scheduled, and they will also be recorded.
Day light saving time starts from 6 Oct (Sunday). Please attend the lectures and labs on time next week.
On Thursday's lecture, we will continue our journal into
Transport Layer
.
The mid-term exam
will take place during the Monday lecture time in
Week 7
(no in-person lecture at that time).
In Week 5, a tutorial will replace the lab. It is not marked. This will serve as useful practice for the mid-term exam. Please look through the questions beforehand and try to solve them by yourself. ALL questions are from past exams, so effectively, it is a sample exam paper. You can ask your tutor to prioritise questions that you find difficult.
The question set
is released.
Finally, the
QILT SES
is closing soon. To encourage participation, the Social Research Centre, which administers the
QILT SES on behalf of the Australian Government, is running a weekly prize draw for all students who complete the survey. So far, one UNSW student has won the minor prize draw.
Enjoy long weekend!

clicking here.

subject: SPAR 3 Announcement
body:
Dear students,
The SPAR (South Pacific Algorithmic Rounds) programming contests series continues this weekend (Saturday 18
th
April),
from 12pm to 5pm at the J17 level 3 labs. This is a series of practice contests for the ICPC, and is suitable for both beginners and experienced competitors. We encourage you to take part and get a taste of competitive programming.
This event is held in conjunction between CSE and CPMSoc. We recommend that you compete in person, as we will provide free pizza
and discuss solutions after the conclusion of the contest. Online participation is also available for those teams unable to attend on campus.
These contests are organised by the South Pacific Competitive Programming Association. You can view the calendar of events and subscribe
for email updates on the
news page
.
You can find the contest rules at
[URL:sppcontests.org]
. Most
notably, this is a team contest for up to three students from the same university or high school.
·
If you competed in an earlier round, please use the same credentials at
[URL:spar.sppcontests.org]
. Teams who perform at a high standard in several SPARs may receive certificates to reward their efforts.
·
You can register your team at
[URL:spar.sppcontests.org]
. Note that only one account is needed per team.
If you don’t have a team, you can:
find a team on the
CPMSoc
Discord server,
make a team on the day, or
compete as an individual.
I hope you choose to participate.
Best of luck,
Raveen de Silva

subject: Reminder to prepare for your Term 1 final exams
body:
Install SEB and take a practice test!
Having trouble viewing this message?
View it online
instead!
Hi Matthew,
You have enrolled in a course in Term 1 that is using the exam platform Inspera and will have an
in-person (face-to-face) invigilated exam
.
In-person attendance at the UNSW Kensington campus for your final exam is a mandatory requirement for this course.
You need to come to campus for your final exam
even if you are enrolled in an online class (such as a lecture or tutorial).
This means that to complete your final exam, you will need to
come to campus, bring your own laptop and install the correct version of the Safe Exam Browser (SEB).
Steps to prepare
1. Prepare your laptop
Don't have one?
2. Get SEB
Download and install
3. Test SEB & Inspera
Take a quick practice test!
Important: Install the latest version of SEB
Please note:
even if you have used SEB for an on-campus invigilated final exam before, you may have an old/unsupported version.
SEB has recently been updated for both Mac and Windows laptops. Please ensure that you install
the latest UNSW compatible version of SEB
on your laptop
prior to your exam, as this will take some time.
Your UNSW final exam may not work if you download SEB from somewhere else.
Computer
SEB version
macOS
3.3.3
Windows
3.8.0
Once you have installed the correct SEB version for your laptop,
make sure you take a practice test
through Inspera to learn the controls and be exam ready. You don't want to be adjusting settings and options during your final exam!
Take a practice test
Important reminders for exam day
Exam day can be stressful and we want to make sure yours is as easy as possible, so please:
Install the correct SEB version on the laptop you will use for your final exam
before
exam day and take a practice test.
Plan your travel
to campus and make sure you arrive on time.
Check your exam location and start time in your
myUNSW Exam Timetable
. The start time of your exam will either be 9.45am or 1.45pm.
Students should be
ready to enter the exam room 15 minutes before these times
. Late arrivals are only allowed into the exam up until 10.30am and 2.30pm respectively.
Check Moodle for details of your exam, including what you need to bring.
Make sure you can access UNSW Wi-Fi on your laptop:
Know your zID and Password
Complete MFA on your phone
You will need to show your SEB login screen on your laptop to the supervising staff to be allowed into the exam room
. You will receive the password for your exam once you are seated inside the exam room.
Please note:
There is an extra 15 minutes added to your exam time to assist with technical issues.
Learn more

Exciting news for students
Having trouble viewing this message?

instead!
Dear Matthew,
We are thrilled to announce the upcoming release of myPlan, our new progression tracking and study planning tool, designed to help you navigate your academic journey with ease at UNSW.
When will I have access to myPlan?
myPlan will launch in two releases to students:
Release 1: Monday 11 November 2024
myPlan will be made available to approximately 35,000 students across 288 programs.

Students in the remaining undergraduate and postgraduate coursework programs will gain access to myPlan from April 2025.

subject: Upcoming Security Society Conference at UNSW
body:
Hi all!
Our yearly student security conference, SCONES, is coming up on the 27th of June at John Niland Scientia, from 10am-5pm.
Just wondering if it's possible for someone to distribute this information to the wider cse student community?
For some context, it's a conference for students, by students, and will feature some very interesting topics both introductory and advanced, including:
* Reverse engineering secure platforms
* Real-world vulnerabilities and bug bounties
* AI pentesting agents
* Drone forensics
* (And a lot more too)
Similarly, it will also feature industry and academia, with panelists and talks from AISA, Google, Atlassian, and full-time staff from CSE. It will also include a capture the flag event with forensics, web security, binary exploitation, and vulnerable hardware
we've designed, etc. And of course, prizes to those that do well in this :)
We've had a lot of effort from our team, and I think there is great value in this for a wide range of curious students - there is free entry too.
More information on tickets and schedules for this can be found here:
[URL:scones.secso.cc]
Let me know if you need more information!
Kind regards,
Tom
--
Thomas McTavish (he/him) | Co-President
UNSW Security Society 2025
University of New South Wales, Sydney NSW 2052, Australia

subject: Co-op Internships Advert - AI Engineering Internship at RBA
body:
The UNSW Co-op Program is offering internships to current students enrolled in an Engineering program (single or dual-degree) and majoring in Software Engineering, Computer Science, Data Science, AI or related
field, who will be in their 2nd year or above in Term 2, 2026.
Get a head start on your career with a one-off 6-month internship with a Co-op Industry Partner. Being awarded an internship by UNSW Co-op is an outstanding addition to your resume and an excellent opportunity
for you to gain ‘real world’ experience prior to graduation. Co-op Internships help you develop your skills and gain clarity on your career goals, by allowing you to apply your university learnings and contribute to meaningful projects, while building your
networks in a professional environment.
Appointed interns must enrol in internship courses in each Term. Successfully completing the internship and coursework for the associated internship course may contribute 12 UOC towards a UNSW degree, for
example:
COMM3101
–
12UOC
(including up to 6 UOC towards each Business School major)
Successful completion would
meet the WIL requirement for Commerce degrees
.
COMM3101 is only available to students who have been offered a Co-op Internship. Once students are offered an internship by one of our industry partners, Co-op requests enrolment
in this course.
Faculty of Arts, Design & Architecture – 6 UOC – ARTS3015 and/or 6 UOC FADA6700
WIL Central (General Education) - 6 UOC – CDEV3400
WIL Central (General Education) - 12 UOC – CDEV3012 - can also be used as 2 Free Electives in a Commerce degree
Use
myPlan
to find out how the UOC from an internship course could contribute to your
degree.
Please note that due to visa and internship course restrictions, these full-time internship opportunities are only available to Australian Citizens and permanent residents who will be enrolled at UNSW in
2026 as undergraduate students.
The successful applicants will be appointed to a 24-week full-time placement with the Industry Partner.
Apply for your Co-op Internship Bursary after being appointed to a Co-op Internship. Bursaries for a 24-week placement total
$21,500
.

Week 8: Risk Management
Week 8: Risk Management
by
Andrew Hingston
- Monday, 6 April 2026, 7:00 AM
I hope that you are enjoying your Easter long-weekend!
A
brief reminder that
Daylight Savings Time ended
in Sydney yesterday
(the first Sunday of April). This involved turning clocks back by one hour
(9:00am became 8:00am).
Your phone should have adjusted
automatically but you may need to adjust other clocks around your home.
Unit
8 focuses on the topic of
Risk Management
. The first part will
attempt to define risk and discuss human bias in the perceptions of risk. Then
we will look at how social security forms an important security net for dealing
with risk as a society. We will then cover the different forms of life insurance,
followed by a brief introduction into estate planning (wills etc.). The final
section will focus on general and health insurances. I have found that most
people do develop a pretty good understanding of general and health insurance
through life experience. However, this Unit will give you a good head start.
The
lectures and quizzes for
Units 8 to 10
will all be made available on the
course website by 5pm this Friday. Students with high initiative might like to
push ahead with their studies to free up some more time at the end of the term
to work on their Financial Plan.
Thanks for submitting your
Adviser
Engagement Assignments
last week. It takes two weeks to mark these
assignments. I will post your grades and some feedback to the course website at
5pm
on
Friday 17 Apr 2026
(Week
9
)
.
How
are you going with your Financial Plan? By the end of this Unit, I urge you to
have make sure that you have written the first draft of the following sections:
1. Current Situation (Unit 1)
2. Life Planning (Unit 2)
3. Financial Strategy (Unit 2)
4. Financial Independence (Unit 3)
5. Career Strategy (Unit 4)
6. Property & Loans (Unit 5)
7. Taxation Planning (Unit 7)
8. Risk Management (Unit 8)
Best wishes
Andrew Hingston
Managing Lecturer

subject: SPAR 2 & 3 Announcement
body:
Dear students,
The SPAR programming contests series continues with two Sprint contests in the coming weeks.
SPAR 2 will be held from 5 pm to 6:45 pm on Wednesday 2
nd
April at the K17 basement.
SPAR 3 will be held from 5 pm to 6:45 pm on Wednesday 9
th
April at the K17 basement.
This is a series of practice contests for the ICPC, and is suitable for both beginners and experienced competitors. We encourage you to take part and get a taste of competitive programming.
This event is held in conjunction between CSE and CPMSoc. We recommend that you compete in person, as we will provide free pizza and discuss solutions after the conclusion of the contest. Online participation is also available for
those teams unable to attend on campus.
These contests are organised by the South Pacific Competitive Programming Association. You can view the calendar of events and subscribe for email updates on the
news page
.
You can find the contest rules at
[URL:sppcontests.org]
. Most notably, this is a team contest for up to three students from the same university or high school.
If you competed in SPAR 1, you should re-use the same login details.
Teams who perform at a high standard in several SPARs may receive certificates to reward their efforts.
Otherwise, y
ou can register your team at
[URL:contest.sppcontests.org]
; note that only one account is needed per team.
If you don’t have a team, you can:
find a team on the
CPMSoc
Discord server,
make a team on the day
, or
compete as an individual.
I hope you choose to participate.
Best of luck,
Raveen de Silva
UNSW ICPC Coach

subject: [COMP2511] Notice: End of Week 2 Updates 🔋
body:
Sai Nair created a notice COMP2511 on Sun 22 September 2024 at 09:43:37 AM.
End of Week 2 Updates 🔋
Hey all,
Hope week 2 treated you alright! We've only got a couple of announcements this week:
Assignment I 🛰️
Both the spec and actual repo you'll be working in have been released for assignment 1, and can be accessed via the
assessments tab
on WebCMS! It's great to see some of you have already begun sinking your teeth into it - if you haven't already, we'd highly recommend getting started early as fully understanding the requirements and executing them with good design takes a while - much longer than you'd expect.
There are a couple of things covered in tute03 which will be useful for the assignment as well, namely some design principles, UML Diagrams and testing with JUnit, which is how your tests should be made for the assignment.
Assignment II Pairs 🐫
Over the next couple of weeks, your tutor will begin organising assignment 2 pairs, and will explain what their process for pairing you up is. Please note:
Pairs must be from the same tutorial
You will be given the opportunity to give preferences for pairs, and while we will try our best to match those preferences, we can't guarantee
all
preferences will be satisfied.
That's about it from us - hope things go smoothly for you with labs and the assignment, and we'll see you all next week 🫡
You can view and reply to the message by
clicking here.
Reply to this email to contact Sai Nair (
[EMAIL]
).

[Notice] 👋🏽 Welcome to COMP1531 + In-person lectures for all
(The

You are welcome to come in person regardless of if you enrolled in the in-person or online stream).
Hi everyone!
Welcome to COMP1531 24T2 🚀🚀🚀🚀 :)
The fundamental aim of COMP1531 is to help you on your transition from being a
solo programmer
into being a
collaborative
software engineer
. We have a teaching staff team of nearly 40, and we're all super excited to get to know you.
If you haven't already, complete the
group preference form
(DUE TOMORROW) and also
introduce yourself
on the forum!
👀 About the course
We have a course structure lecture for you to check out! If you watch it at 1.5x it's only less than 15 minutes. It's a great one to watch before our first lecture on Monday.
👔 Getting ready for week 1!
Here is a quick summary of getting ready for week 1.
Lectures
Our first lecture is on Monday, 11 am - 1 pm
. It will be both
in person
in
Physics Theatre (K-K14-19)
and online (
LINK HERE
).
Tutorial / Lab
Your week 1 tute/lab class is a one-hour tutorial and a two-hour lab. Make sure you have looked at
where your tutorials and labs
are (and if you're not sure, email your tutor—
emails here
).
There are lab questions to complete in week 1! We will release all labs this weekend
on the course website
.
However:
Week 1 lab questions aren't worth anything - we mark them for practice, but don't actually count your marks.
Week 1 lab is due on MONDAY of week 2. So for late enrolments you have week 1 weekend to sort that out.
Your tutorial and lab material for week 1 can be found
here
(to be released this weekend)!
During your class, your tutor will finalise your group allocations for the major project.
See you soon!
It's a pretty relaxed week and things will really start to pick up next Friday where we'll send another notice. The main update on Friday is going to be information about the very first & easy milestone your group will have for the major project.
If you have any questions you can just post on the forum.
See you in the first lecture 😎😎😎
🐕

subject: FW: Poster for Day of AI <> UNSW CSE Competition
body:
Hi All,
Good Morning & Happy Friday !
😊
The Day of AI Australia is a full day of highly interactive lessons that introduce the basics of AI literacy to students in Years 5 to 10. The Day of AI has been developed by the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and i2Learning.
Day of AI Australia partners UNSW's School of Computer Science and Engineering, CSIRO, and CS in Schools to tailor content for Australian students and teachers. In 2023 more than 18,000 Australian students participated in the Day of AI.
In 2024, Day of AI Australia is partnering with UNSW’s School of Computer Science and Engineering (CSE) for an exciting opportunity for CSE students to develop a participant challenge as a key
component of the Day’s activities. All details are available in the PDF.
Thanks everyone,
Jake Renzella PhD, AFHEA

subject: Student ID card required for final exams on campus
body:
Important update
Having trouble viewing this message?
View it online
instead!
Hi Matthew, make sure you have your student ID card
From Term 3 2025 you will no longer be able to sit your final exams on campus without
physical photo identification
. Electronic IDs will not be accepted. Your physical photo ID should be your UNSW student ID card - it will be scanned at the exam venue as part of a new attendance checking process.
It is a requirement of the

subject: Changes to Special Consideration: Short Extensions
body:
Important update for students
Having trouble viewing this message?
View it online
instead!
Changes to Special Consideration: Short Extensions
Hi Matthew,
We’re excited to share some positive changes to our Special Consideration process that aim to provide even more support for our students facing unexpected challenges.
From Term 1 2024, we are introducing
Short Extensions
, a process designed to offer you additional support when you have short term circumstances impacting your ability to submit your assessment on time.
What is a Short Extension?
A Short Extension allows you to apply for extra time on your assessment deadline without the need to provide supporting documentation, offering immediate approval during brief, life-disrupting events.
Why are we making these changes?
Our primary goal is to enhance your student experience by providing immediate support and approval when you are experiencing challenges to submit on time. We understand that life can be unpredictable, and these changes
are meant to streamline the process, making it less stressful for you to request help when you need it.
How does it work?
Course Convenors decide whether any assessments in their courses are eligible for a Short Extension prior to the start of each term.
The availability of Short Extensions for eligible assessments will be communicated in your
Course Outlines
and/or Learning Management System (LMS) eg.
Moodle
.
If a Short Extension is available for your assessment, you may apply without documentation via the Short Extension student portal, which you can access from the login page of the
Special Consideration portal
. Once you have submitted your request, it will be automatically approved if submitted before the assessment deadline, and you will receive a confirmation email with the revised due date for your assessment.
If a Short Extension is not available, you may apply for Special Consideration via the
Special Consideration portal
, however, you will be required to provide sufficient and appropriate supporting documentation as per the standard Special Consideration process.
It is important to note that the availability of a Short Extension will vary based on individual courses and is at the discretion of the Course Authority.
Where can I find more information?
You can find further information about Short Extensions on the
Special Consideration
webpage. We've also included information on other changes to Special Consideration, including a deadline of 3 working days to apply for Special Consideration after your assessment due date, and the removal of 'Fit to Submit'.

COMP1531 –
Announcements
[Notice] End of week 1 updates
(We will have panel discussion on teamwork. If you have any question about teamwork,
please fill in this form
and our panels will answer them in the discussion lecture.)
Hi everyone! 😻
Now that we're wrapping up week 1, it's a great time to summarise the key things that happened this week so you can easily feel on top it.
🫡 Week 1 recap
The key things that happened in week 1 are:
Week 1 lecture slides and recordings
can be found on this page
or
on the course website
.
This week, we talked about javascript and git solo usage.
We will talk more about git (team usage & branches) in week 2. To avoid confusions, I did not rush to finish everything about git. What you have learnt so far is enough to support you for week 1.
some further discussions:
Accessing Object Properties
,
Arrays and Object
.
Week 1 tutorial and lab content links
can be found on this page
.
Tutorial solutions will be released on Friday night on a "solution" branch.
Labs are due the following Monday (so you have 3 days to finish lab01)
Lab solutions
are released on a solution branch on Monday evening after due date
Lab marks
are returned to you the following Friday (in 7 days from now)
Project groups will be finalised by end of tomorrow (if you're unsure about any of this email your project tutor)
Project will be released to you by Monday of week 2.
If you're still a bit unsure about any of the software or tools to setup for the course check out the
getting started guide
. We have added
new video recordings
showing you how to set up.
We have created
a help sessions calendar
that you can download.
🔥Class reschedule
Week 3 Monday is a public holiday - which means that we cannot have a lecture or tutorials on that day.
We will instead reschedule the lecture to an online-only discussion talking about teamwork. It will be week 2, Thursday from 4 pm.
Put your questions about teamwork in this form, and our panel will answer them in the discussion!
Deadline for submitting your questions is next Monday 11:59pm.
Your tutor will organise another time for your tute/lab classes.
🐇Project groups
During your week 1 class your tutor will have finalised your project groups in your lab time.
A reminder that whilst you aren't required to communicate via the Microsoft teams channel we make for you, it's important to note that if teamwork disputes arise or you make claims about other team members contributions later in the course - the
only
places we will look to for evidence of how you interact with your group are:
The project check-ins and iteration demonstrations
Gitlab contributions (code you push)
Peer reviews at the end of the iteration
Any UNSW emails sent between group members
Conversations that occur on
Microsoft teams only
If we need to analyse group communication, we simply won't be able to look at things like Facebook, Discord, Slack, etc, as these are out of our control and are open to tampering etc that make it unfair to consider reasonable evidence. However, anything in your Microsoft teams chat we'll happily take into account. If you're uncertain about anything here or aren't agreeing in your group how to communicate, talk to your tutor in week 2 and they will help you figure it all out! :)
If you don't have your group sorted, or weren't added to your groups Microsoft teams chat, please email your tutor ASAP.
🪜Project release & Iteration 0
By week 2 Monday morning, a group project repository will be created for you that
you can find here
.
The first stage of the project is iteration 0 and you have 5 days next week to complete it. It is due next Friday night (see the spec when it is released)
It's
extremely
easy and could be mostly done in the lab time next week.
Please watch the introductory video at the top of the spec - it explains everything.
It's important that you start chatting to your group on MS teams first thing Monday - some of you have labs later in the week, and even though iteration 0 is small, you don't want to be pressed up against a wall trying to rush last minute.
Over the coming days, there may continue to be slight changes to your group primarily due to students potentially dropping the course before census date. Your tutors will always support you and find solutions that will make things OK :)
🐸Last Things!
Next week we'll do some interesting things on git, project management, testing, teamwork.
Sometimes we push updates to your repos (e.g. labs or project) that may appear as merge requests in the left hand sidebar. Keep an eye out for it. Your tutor(s) can always help out if you're confused.
Other than that, have a great weekend!!!
(End of week 0 updates)
---

subject: [COMP6713] Notice: Assignment: Response to questions (1/n)
body:
Dipankar Srirag 📸 updated a notice COMP6713 on Thu 05 March 2026 at 11:32:30 AM.
Assignment: Response to questions (1/n)
Hello everyone,
It is great to see that you started working on the assignment, and have similar questions, particularly around what counts as
training data
and what external resources are allowed.
For this assignment, the dataset we provided (data.csv) should be used to build any
models, statistics, or heuristics
that help you identify which words in a sentence should be replaced. You are free to use any reasonable method for this part, but
please do not use large pretrained transformer models such as BERT, GPT, etc
.
Regarding
pretrained embeddings and lexical resources
, you are allowed to use tools such as
GloVe, gensim embeddings, spaCy (including models like en_core_web_sm), NLTK and WordNet
identify suitable replacement words
or compute semantic similarity. However, they should not be used as additional training data for your model.
Please also make sure that your submission is self-sufficient and runs without any manual changes to it when running automatic tests. Your submission should contain only:
zid.py
zid.pdf
packaged together in zid.zip.
Finally, we have uploaded a small set of unit tests on Moodle. The unit tests are provided
only for your own qualitative testing
, and you are allowed to add your examples to the file. You can use them to check whether your approach produces reasonable outputs. The automatic marking tests and the corresponding setup is not being
shared.
Hope this helps clarify things. If you still have questions, please feel free to post on the forum. All the best!
Regards,
Dipankar

subject: Learn how to protect yourself against scams 🔒
body:
Stay vigilant and report suspicious activity
Having trouble viewing this message?
View it online
instead!
Matthew, do you know how to spot a scam?
Scammers can strike when you least expect them, tricking you into handing over money or personal details. Scammers are even pretending to be UNSW, resulting in some students losing thousands of dollars.
Take a minute to learn how to identify and respond to scams.
Recognise the red flags
🚩
It could be a scam if you're asked to pay your tuition fees:
Over the phone
On social media
By direct bank transfer
UNSW will never ask you to pay your fees via any method other than myUNSW.
Be aware that any person or service promising a discount on your fees is attempting to scam you and take your money.
Authentic emails from UNSW will:
Direct you to pay tuition fees on myUNSW  ✅
Come from an email address ending in @unsw.edu.au  ✅
Suspect a scam?
Take these steps to protect yourself, your family and other students.
1. Stop
Do not provide any information or money.
Hang up suspicious phone calls.
Do not click links in suspicious emails.
You can verify that a message has come from UNSW and not a scammer by contacting
The Nucleus: Student Hub
.
2. Report
Report suspected scams to
UNSW Protective Services
. Don't be afraid to report or seek advice. Speaking up protects you, your family and other students.
3. Get support
If you have been targeted, contact
Psychology and Wellness
or a
Student Support Advisor
.
Discover the types of scams that target UNSW students
Learn more about how to spot these scams, and what to do if you're targeted.

Some updates as we go into the end of the week.
Please see the allocations for the Week 3 problem submissions, under the “assignment” Week 3 Workshop Question Allocation. Group compositions have also been attached. These are due on Gradescope by the end of Friday, Week 4.

The M15 workshop has been rescheduled next week. It is now being held on Wednesday 8th October, from 12 - 3pm in
Randwick 201
. This was the best of many bad options unfortunately.
We have had many questions about the “Project - Feedback Week x” objects on Gradescope. These are completely optional, and are intended to give you a way to submit for running feedback on the project. They are not assessed and
their contents are not tied to the marking of all project deliverables. You should still submit all project deliverables in the correct submission box by their due dates.
Workshop 1 marks have now been published on Gradescope. Please email if you have any concerns about your mark, including if you believe the late penalty was applied incorrectly.

review of results
, except that you should email your enquiry to the course account. The link also contains the unacceptable grounds for review. You
must
provide a clear reason for requesting a regrading and include supporting evidence. Requests without adequate justification or evidence will be declined.
General feedback on submissions from Workshop 1 will be published on Moodle soon. You can view your own personal feedback on Gradescope.

link to the logs, via providing a full transcript, or screenshots. The use of generative AI tools contrary to these instructions will result in plagiarism allegations.

How to switch to MATH1131
How to switch to MATH1131
by
Wolfgang Schief
- Tuesday, 20 February 2024, 10:05 AM
Dear Students,
At the start of the term, many students are unsure about whether MATH1141 Higher Mathematics 1A is the right course for them.  Often the best way to figure this out is to try MATH1141 for a week or two and then make a decision about whether to continue with
MATH1141 or switch to MATH1131 based on how you find this.  Note that although many degrees do not require MATH1141 over MATH1131, some do, so you may need advice from the Nucleus about whether you can make this switch and remain in your current degree.
Although it is no longer possible for you to change courses yourself on myUNSW, it is still possible to do so with the help of the Nucleus, and although the deadline for switching to MATH1131 is late in Week 4, it's best if this decision is made by the end
of Week 2.
If you have decided you would prefer to take MATH1131 this term, you should complete the following form:
Late
Enrolment Request Higher/Lower Course Variation webform
If you need advice before making this decision, do not hesitate to contact me.
Kind regards,
Wolfgang

subject: ICPC Regional Finals Mirror Contest Announcement
body:
Dear students,
UNSW is hosting the South Pacific ICPC Regional Finals next weekend, on Sunday 20
th
October. Alongside the official competition for teams who qualified in the Preliminary contest on Saturday 31
st
August, we will
also be holding a unofficial mirror contest which is open to all students. We encourage you to take part and challenge yourself against the best teams across Australia, New Zealand and the Pacific. The contest will run from 10am-3pm Sydney time.
We will be hosting a live site at the J17 level 3 labs, in partnership with CPMSoc. We recommend that you compete in person as:
you’ll get a free lunch
you can attend the closing ceremony, where the judges will discuss solutions to the problems
you’ll get a free dinner.
Non-UNSW students are also welcome to attend, so feel free to invite your friends from other institutions. High school students are also welcome to attend, and to bring teachers and parents
if desired.
Please register your attendance at
[URL:forms.office.com]
, to help us plan catering. Every person attending must complete the form (not just one entry per team).
Online participation is also available, for those teams unable to attend on campus. The contest will be available on
DOMJudge
, where you will be able to register, view the problem set and scoreboard, and submit your solutions.
COMP4128 students can earn bonus marks for participating or volunteering in this contest.
Registration:
No formal registration on
icpc.global
is needed, as the mirror contest is entirely unofficial.
Teams with an existing DOMjudge account [ORDER_ID] the SPAR contests can
log in here
. Accounts from the Preliminary Contest should not be re-used.
New teams can
self-register
on DOMJudge.
Rules:
Teams should have 3 people if possible. We will accept teams with fewer people, but not more.
You do not have to stay in the same team from the Preliminary contest.
I will not find teammates for individuals or pairs for this contest. You should arrange your own teams, for example through the CPMSoc Discord server or in person on the day.
Teams may use as many printed notes as you like, but no electronic material other than language documentation:
C/C++:
[URL:sppcontests.org]
Java:
[URL:sppcontests.org]
Python 3:
[URL:sppcontests.org]
Teams may use pens, paper and small white boards, for developing and discussing solutions among themselves.
The only applications teams may use during the contest is a code editor, a terminal to run and compile code, and a web browser but only to access the contest server and online documentation.
Teams may not access any electronic copies of previously written code or templates during the contest.
Teams should not discuss the problems with people outside the team during to the contest, and any questions must be sent through the DOMJudge clarification system.
Electronic devices (phones, tablets etc) should not be used during the contest.
Only one person from each team can use the keyboard at a time. This can be achieved by the team sharing a single workstation. However, if social distancing requirements make this
infeasible, teams should self-regulate to make sure only one person is on a keyboard at a time.
To solve a problem, teams should submit source code through the DOMJudge system written in C, C++, Java or Python 3. The only feedback teams will receive is "compile error", "runtime
error", "time limit exceeded", "wrong answer" or "correct".
Teams are ranked by who has solved the most problems, plus the aggregate of the time taken to solve each problem (the time in minutes between the start of the contest and when the
correct solution was submitted, plus a 20 minute penalty for each incorrect submission).
The contest server will evaluate any solution submitted in the five hours from the start of the contest. The scoreboard will stop updating with
1
hour
left in the contest, and the full scoreboard will be revealed shortly after the end of the contest.
Any teams found to be cheating will be removed from the contest server.
For more information on the mirror contest, please contact me by
email
or post in the CPMSoc
Discord server
. Information about future contests can be found at
[URL:sppcontests.org]
.
I hope you choose to participate.
Best of luck,
Raveen De Silva
UNSW ICPC Coach
